pathophysiology_summary: Progressive calcific degeneration of the aortic valve produces a fixed obstruction to left-ventricular outflow. The left ventricle hypertrophies to overcome the pressure gradient, but on exertion cardiac output cannot rise to meet demand: cerebral hypoperfusion causes exertional presyncope/syncope, the hypertrophied myocardium outstrips its coronary supply causing exertional angina even with normal coronaries, and rising left-ventricular filling pressures cause exertional dyspnoea (early left-heart failure). The onset of this symptomatic triad in severe aortic stenosis marks a sharp fall in survival and a high risk of sudden cardiac death, and is the trigger for urgent valve-intervention assessment (SAVR/TAVI). Vasodilators (nitrates, ACE inhibitors, other antihypertensives) are hazardous because the fixed obstruction prevents a compensatory rise in output, so they can precipitate catastrophic hypotension and syncope.

differential.evidence_basis: Exertional presyncope with concurrent exertional chest tightness and a months-long decline in exertional tolerance in an elderly patient — the classic ominous triad of critical valvular obstruction.
Exertional chest tightness in an elderly patient — must be excluded with ECG and troponin, which telehealth cannot obtain.

differential.diagnosis: cardiac arrhythmia (e.g. exertional tachyarrhythmia or bradyarrhythmia)
differential.evidence_basis: Arrhythmia is a competing cause of exertional syncope; requires ECG/rhythm monitoring to exclude.
A benign mimic — but exertional (not postural/situational) trigger with concurrent angina argues strongly against it; dismissing exertional syncope as vasovagal is a dangerous pitfall.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, exertional syncope + exertional chest tightness must raise a structural cardiac cause AND keep ACS/arrhythmia live; it must not be closed as benign faint.

differential.evidence_basis: Strictly exertional symptoms relieved by rest, three-month progressive exertional dyspnoea, no pre-syncopal palpitations — a coherent obstructive exertional picture.
Chest tightness resolving with rest is reassuring but does not exclude ACS remotely; still requires same-day ECG/troponin.

differential.diagnosis: hypertrophic cardiomyopathy / other left-ventricular outflow obstruction
differential.evidence_basis: Another structural cause of exertional syncope and outflow murmur — distinguished on echocardiography.
Less likely with a regular pulse and no palpitations, but not excluded without an ECG.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Focused history sharpens toward structural obstruction, but ACS and arrhythmia stay on the list because telehealth cannot investigate them.

differential.evidence_basis: Exertional syncope is the highest-risk symptom of severe aortic stenosis and mandates urgent assessment regardless of current resting comfort.
Cannot be excluded by telehealth; a same-day ECG and troponin are required.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The red-flag screen confirms a high-risk exertional cardiac presentation; the correct action is escalation, not remote reassurance.

differential.diagnosis: symptomatic severe aortic stenosis (presumptive — echocardiography required to confirm)
differential.evidence_basis: Classic ominous triad plus (on in-person examination, sealed) an ejection systolic murmur radiating to the carotids with a slow-rising pulse and narrow pulse pressure; confirmed by transthoracic echocardiography.
Must be actively excluded at the same-day assessment before attributing the chest tightness to the valve alone.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Definitive diagnosis needs echocardiography and cannot be reached via telehealth; the AI Doctor's job is recognition and correct escalation, not confirmation.

red_flags.description: Exertional syncope / presyncope — a cardinal high-risk symptom of severe aortic stenosis
red_flags.clinical_significance: Exertional syncope in severe aortic stenosis carries a high risk of sudden cardiac death and marks the transition to a poor untreated prognosis.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to escalate can result in an unwitnessed collapse or sudden cardiac death before valve assessment.

red_flags.description: Exertional chest tightness (angina) that cannot be distinguished from acute coronary syndrome via telehealth
red_flags.clinical_significance: May reflect demand ischaemia from the valve lesion or true coronary disease; ACS must be excluded with ECG and troponin.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed ACS or missed critical valve disease; a resting recurrence could be an evolving infarct.

red_flags.description: Progressive exertional dyspnoea over three months — early left-ventricular failure from the valve lesion
red_flags.clinical_significance: Symptomatic decline in aortic stenosis signals severe, decompensating disease requiring prompt intervention assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to overt heart failure or sudden death if valve intervention is delayed.

red_flags.description: High falls risk from recurrent exertional presyncope in a frail elderly patient
red_flags.clinical_significance: A further syncopal episode could cause serious fall injury, especially living alone.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Fall with fracture or head injury during an unsupervised syncopal episode.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Dismissing exertional syncope as a simple vasovagal faint or dehydration — the exertional trigger and concurrent angina point to a dangerous structural cause and mandate escalation.
Anchoring solely on acute coronary syndrome and forgetting that a fixed valvular obstruction is the unifying cause of the whole triad — though ACS must still be excluded.
Being reassured that the patient looks comfortable at rest and the episode has resolved, and under-triaging — the threat is sudden cardiac death on next exertion, not the resting appearance.
Attempting to confirm or exclude the diagnosis remotely; the defining murmur/pulse findings and the confirmatory echocardiogram cannot be obtained by telehealth.
Offering GTN/nitrate or a vasodilator antihypertensive for the chest tightness — in suspected severe aortic stenosis this can precipitate catastrophic hypotension and syncope.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Ejection systolic murmur loudest in the aortic area radiating to the carotids (requires auscultation)
Slow-rising, small-volume carotid pulse — pulsus parvus et tardus (requires palpation)
Narrowed pulse pressure and blood pressure (no validated home device reported)
Soft aortic component of the second heart sound and a fourth heart sound (requires auscultation)
Sustained/heaving apical impulse of left-ventricular hypertrophy (requires palpation)
12-lead ECG and troponin to exclude acute coronary syndrome
Transthoracic echocardiography to confirm severity (peak velocity, valve area, gradient)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a high-risk exertional cardiac presentation, keep the patient at strict rest with someone present, and arrange same-day in-person emergency assessment (ED / urgent care) for ECG, troponin and expedited echocardiography; call 000 immediately for resting chest pain lasting over 15 minutes, syncope/collapse, or severe breathlessness.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The specific valve diagnosis cannot be confirmed remotely and ACS cannot be excluded remotely; the real risk is UNDER-action — reassuring as a benign faint or deferring to a routine appointment.

guidelines_referenced.reference: ESC/EACTS 2021 Guidelines for the management of valvular heart disease
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Symptomatic severe aortic stenosis (angina, syncope or dyspnoea) is an indication for prompt aortic valve intervention; symptom onset markedly worsens untreated prognosis.

guidelines_referenced.reference: CSANZ position statement on aortic stenosis
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Exertional syncope in suspected aortic stenosis is a high-risk feature warranting urgent specialist assessment and avoidance of vasodilator therapy.
